calendar.matters:
MASTER CALENDAR COURT
Off Calendar Jul-08-2025 Vacated

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Sep-11-2024 Set For Trial On Aug-04-25 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 206

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Jul-02-2024 Continued To Sep-25-24 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

Defendants' Motion To Strike Disgorgement Fees From Plaintiff'S Complaint
Off Calendar Jun-14-2024 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Andrew Browning, Esq., Tel. 415.348.0348, appearing in person for the plaintiff 2710 Sutter Ventures, LLC and Sutter Partner Holdings LLC;
Inna Nytochka, Esq., Tel. 415.625.9245, appearing in person for the defendants Dowling & Marquez, Curtis Dowling, and Jak Marquez.

Hearing re: DEFENDANTS DOWLING & MARQUEZ, CURTIS DOWLING, JAK MARQUEZ'S Motion To Strike Disgorgement Fees From Plaintiff's Complaint.

The matter is contested. Argument is held. At the conclusion of argument, the Court adopted the tentative ruling as follows:

Defendants' motion to strike is granted with twenty days leave to amend.

The notice of motion substantially complies with CRC 3.1110(a) and sufficiently states "the nature of the order being sought and the grounds for the issuance of the order." (Id.)

Defendants seek to strike plaintiff's prayer for disgorgement of attorney's fees. (Complaint, pg. 6, prayer, par. b.) Plaintiff correctly notes that "[d]isgorgement of fees may be an appropriate remedy for an attorney's breach of fiduciary duty." (Slovensky v. Friedman (2006) 142 Cal.App.4th 1518, 1535.) But not every breach of fiduciary duty does not warrants that remedy. (See Mardirossian & Associates, Inc. v. Ersoff (2007) 153 Cal.App.4th 257, 278 ["Although the breach of a rule of professional conduct may warrant a forfeiture of fees, forfeiture is not automatic but depends on the egregiousness of the violation."]; Pringle v. La Chapelle (1999) 73 Cal.App.4th 1000, 1005-1006 [neither the Bus. & Prof. Code nor the Rules of Prof. Conduct provide for deprivation of fees whenever a rule of professional conduct is violated; whether forfeiture of the right to collect fees is required depends on the egregious nature of the violation].)

In this case, plaintiff's allegations sound in negligence and plaintiff does not clearly allege how defendants breached their duties of loyalty and honesty nor how the equities support the remedy of forfeiture. (Complaint, pars. 11-13, 18, 24-25.) Plaintiff argues that: "Defendants doubled down on their negligence and advised Plaintiffs to file an appeal knowing that the underlying unlawful detainer action was defective" (Opposition 2:25-26.) The complaint, however, does not clearly allege that defendants counseled plaintiff to pursue the appeal despite knowing that the unlawful detainer was defective. Plaintiff has leave to amend to plead facts showing a breach "sufficiently egregious to require forfeiture." (Mardirossian & Associates, Inc., 153 Cal.App.4th at 278; see also Sheppard, Mullin, Richter & Hampton, LLP v. J-M Manufacturing Co., Inc. (2018) 6 Cal.5th 59, 90 ["The remedy of fee forfeiture presupposes that a lawyer's clear and serious violation of a duty to a client destroys or severely impairs the client-lawyer relationship and thereby the justification of the lawyer's claim to compensation."].)

The Court to review the proposed order submitted by the prevailing party.

Judge: Rochelle C. East. Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda. Deputy: Jaime. Matter not reported.
(302/RCE)
